question: Who developed the Android operating system?
description: Android is an open-source mobile OS developed by Google. The first Android device was launched in 2008.

question: Android is based on which kernel?
description: Android is built on top of the Linux kernel, which sits at the bottom of the architecture and handles device drivers, memory management, and power management.

question: Which language is primarily used to write Android applications?
description: Android applications are usually written in Java or Kotlin. Other languages like C#, Python, and Dart can also be used via frameworks.

question: What language is used for Android UI/UX layouts?
description: Android UI is defined using XML (eXtensible Markup Language) in the res/layout/ folder.

question: What is API Level in Android?
option_b: An integer that uniquely identifies the framework API revision
option_c: The processor speed of the device
description: API Level is an integer value that uniquely identifies the framework API revision offered by a version of the Android platform. For example, Android 13 = API 33.

question: Which Android version has the codename 'Tiramisu'?
description: Android 13 has the codename Tiramisu with API level 33.

question: How many layers are there in the Android architecture?
description: Android architecture has 5 layers: Linux Kernel, Native Libraries, Android Runtime, Application Framework, and Applications.

question: Which layer of the Android architecture is responsible for device drivers and power management?
description: The Linux Kernel is the heart of the architecture and is responsible for device drivers, power management, memory management, and hardware access (camera, keypad, display).

question: What is the role of DVM (Dalvik Virtual Machine)?
option_a: To compile Java code into bytecode
option_b: To run Android applications, optimized for mobile devices
description: DVM (Dalvik Virtual Machine) is responsible for running Android applications. It is similar to JVM but optimized for mobile devices — consumes less memory and provides fast performance.

question: Which native library is responsible for database support in Android?
description: SQLite is the native library used for database support in Android. WebKit is for browser, FreeType for fonts, and SSL for internet security.

question: Which Application Framework component allows apps to display alerts to the user?
description: Notifications Manager allows applications to display alerts and notifications to the user.

question: Which build system does Android Studio use?
description: Android Studio uses Gradle as its build system.

question: Which file describes all the components of an Android app?
description: AndroidManifest.xml (in the manifests/ folder) describes all the components of your Android application.

question: Which res/ subfolder contains the launcher icons for the app?
description: mipmap/ contains the launcher icons. drawable is for general images, layout for UI files, and values for strings/colors.

question: How many times must you tap 'Build number' to enable Developer Options?
description: On an Android device, you tap Build number seven (7) times in Settings > About phone to unlock Developer Options, then enable USB Debugging.

question: How many main components are there in an Android application?
description: There are 4 main components: Activities, Services, Broadcast Receivers, and Content Providers.

question: Which Android component handles background processing?
description: Services handle background processing associated with an application — they don't have a UI.

question: Which component handles communication between the Android OS and applications?
description: Broadcast Receivers handle communication between the Android OS and applications (e.g., battery low, incoming SMS).

question: What does a Fragment represent in Android?
option_b: A behavior or portion of UI within an Activity
description: Fragments represent a behavior or portion of user interface in an Activity. They allow modular UI design.

question: Which year was the first Android device launched?
description: The first Android device was launched in 2008.

question: What is an Activity in Android?
option_b: A single screen in Android containing all UI components
description: An Activity is the single screen in Android. It contains all UI components in a single screen.

question: How many lifecycle methods control an Android Activity?
description: Android Activity Lifecycle is controlled by 7 methods: onCreate, onStart, onResume, onPause, onStop, onRestart, onDestroy.

question: Which method is called on the initial creation of the Activity?
description: onCreate() is called on initial creation of the Activity. It is responsible for constructing views, binding data, and managing/restoring state from its bundle.

question: Which method fires when the Activity is ready to receive user input?
description: onResume() fires when the activity has been created, started, and is ready to receive user input. The activity will start interacting with the user after this method completes.

question: Which method is called when the Activity is visible but not interactive (e.g., a popup is shown)?
description: onPause() is triggered whenever the activity is visible but not interactive — for example when a popup appears or when transitioning to another activity.

question: Which method is called when the Activity is no longer visible?
description: onStop() is called when the activity is not visible on the screen anymore.

question: Which method is invoked after the Activity has been stopped, right before starting up again?
description: onRestart() is invoked after the activity has been stopped, right before starting up again. After this, onStart() is called.

question: Which method is called before the Activity is destroyed?
description: onDestroy() is called before the activity is destroyed — usually as a result of finish() or pressing the Back button. Called at most once per activity.

question: What is the correct sequence when an app is first created?
description: When an app is first created, the sequence is: onCreate → onStart → onResume.

question: What is the correct sequence when the app is closed (Back button pressed)?
description: When the app is closed or Back is pressed: onPause → onStop → onDestroy.

question: When a popup appears partially obscuring the screen, which method is called?
description: When the screen is partially obscured by a popup, only onPause() is called — NOT onStop, because the activity is still visible. When the popup closes, onResume() is called.

question: When the screen sleeps and the app goes to the background, which methods are called in order?
description: When sending the app to background or screen sleeps, the sequence is onPause → onStop. When it wakes up: onRestart → onStart → onResume.

question: When the screen wakes up after sleeping, which method is called FIRST?
description: When the screen wakes up after the app was sent to background, the sequence is: onRestart → onStart → onResume. So onRestart() is called first.

question: Which lifecycle methods are called AT MOST ONCE per application run?
description: onCreate and onDestroy are called at most once each time the application is run. The others (onPause, onStop, onRestart, onStart, onResume) may be called many times during the lifecycle.

question: Which methods can be called MANY times during the lifecycle?
description: onPause, onStop, onRestart, onStart, and onResume may be called many times during the lifecycle as the user navigates in and out of the activity.

question: When the app is killed in the background to free resources, which method is the LAST to be called?
description: When the app is killed in the background to free resources, onStop() is the last to be called before termination. The app remains in history; selecting it from history starts again from onCreate().

question: When MainActivity calls NextActivity, what is the order of method calls?
description: When MainActivity calls NextActivity: Main.onPause → Next.onCreate → Next.onStart → Next.onResume → Main.onStop. The new activity must be at least started before the old one can stop.

question: After onPause(), what are the possible next methods?
description: After onPause(), the next method is either onStop() (if the activity becomes invisible) or onResume() (if it becomes interactive again, e.g., popup closed).

question: Which lifecycle methods have 'Kill After = Yes' (the system can kill the app after them)?
description: onPause, onStop, and onDestroy have 'Kill After = Yes' — meaning the system can terminate the app process after these methods if memory is needed.

question: What triggers onDestroy() most commonly?
option_b: Explicit call to finish() or pressing the Back button
description: onDestroy() is usually the result of an explicit call to finish() from within the activity, or when the Back button is pressed.
